title: SriLankan Airlines and Colombo CMB: An Indian-Ocean Hub
dek: Colombo sits midway between the Gulf and Southeast Asia, and SriLankan Airlines uses that geography to feed Indian-Ocean traffic. A look at the carrier's network, fleet and the pressures ahead in 2025.
body:
SriLankan Airlines and its home base at Bandaranaike International Airport (CMB) in Colombo continue to play an outsized role in linking South Asia, the Gulf and Southeast Asia, despite the carrier operating a comparatively compact fleet. Built around Airbus widebody and narrowbody aircraft, the airline's network leans on Colombo's geographic position roughly midway between the Gulf and Southeast Asia to feed connecting traffic across the Indian Ocean. In 2025 that hub role remains central to how travellers move between India, the Middle East and points further east.

A network shaped by geography

SriLankan, a member of the oneworld alliance, concentrates much of its flying on dense short-haul links to Indian cities alongside longer routes to the Gulf, Southeast Asia and a handful of long-haul destinations. The carrier's Airbus A330 widebodies handle higher-demand and longer sectors, while A320-family jets, including A321neo aircraft, serve regional markets such as India, the Maldives and the Gulf.

Colombo's appeal as a connecting point rests on proximity. Many Indian cities sit within a short hop of CMB, making one-stop routings via Colombo competitive for passengers heading onward to Southeast Asia or the Maldives, and giving the airline a natural feeder role for the region.

Pressures and the road ahead

The airline has faced well-documented financial strain and has been the subject of a long-running restructuring and privatisation effort led by the Sri Lankan government. Fleet size, route profitability and capital constraints all weigh on how aggressively the carrier can grow, and any expansion tends to be measured rather than rapid.

  • Colombo's mid-ocean location supports one-stop India-to-Southeast-Asia routings.
  • The fleet mixes A330 widebodies with A320-family narrowbodies.
  • Restructuring and ownership questions continue to shape network decisions.

For the wider Indian-Ocean region, a stable SriLankan matters: CMB is a natural bridge, and the carrier's fleet and route choices will influence connectivity for Sri Lanka, the Maldives and southern India alike in the years ahead.
